errors: [Age threshold set to 40 years, which is lower than most breast‑cancer screening guidelines that recommend starting routine mammography at 50 (or 45 in some guidelines); Lookback period for mammogram set to 1 year, whereas guidelines typically recommend a 2‑year interval for routine screening.]

[LookBack interval incorrectly set to 1 month instead of the clinically appropriate 10‑year interval for colonoscopy screening; the logic for determining a colonoscopy within the look‑back period is therefore incorrect.]

The logic excludes patients who have osteopenia or an osteoporotic fracture from the screening population. According to USPSTF guidelines, women 65 years or older should be screened for osteoporosis with DXA regardless of prior osteopenia or fracture history. Additionally, the “Has Osteoporotic Fracture” expression only checks that the clinicalStatus is not null, which may not reliably identify fracture conditions.

[HbA1c threshold set to ≥9 g% is clinically unreasonable for a diabetes diagnosis (typical diagnostic threshold is ≥6.5 % or 48 mmol/mol); the unit “g%” is incorrect – HbA1c is expressed as a percentage, not grams per percent.]
The CDS logic does not fully align with USPSTF recommendations. Specifically:

The age range for inclusion (60–75 years) does not align with current average‑risk colorectal cancer screening guidelines, which recommend screening starting at 45 years. Additionally, the logic excludes patients who had any colorectal screening observation in the past 2 years; this interval is too short for colonoscopy (recommended every 10 years) and may incorrectly flag patients who had a colonoscopy within that period as needing screening.

The age threshold of 30 years is not consistent with current HIV screening guidelines, which recommend screening for all adults aged 15–65 (or 18–65 in some guidelines). The logic should be adjusted to include the appropriate age range.

The BMI threshold for “Is Obese” is set to ≥35 kg/m², whereas clinical guidelines define obesity as BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² (class I obesity). This threshold is higher than the standard definition and may exclude many patients who should be screened.
[Age range mismatch: the logic uses 30‑65 years for eligibility, while the recommendation and rationale state screening for women aged 21‑65 years.]

The age threshold in the **In Demographic** expression is set to 75 years (≥ 75), whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends falls‑risk screening for community‑dwelling adults **65 years or older**. This mismatch makes the clinical trigger too restrictive and not aligned with the guideline.

The age threshold in the logic is set to 25 years (In Demographic: age ≥ 25), whereas the rationale states that USPSTF recommends screening for adults 18 years or older. This mismatch between the implemented age criterion and the cited guideline recommendation is clinically inconsistent.

The age range in the ELM uses 55–75 years for eligibility, whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends AAA screening for men aged 65–75 years who have ever smoked. This lower age bound (55) is clinically incorrect.

The logic for “Needs AAA Screening” does not include a check for male gender, so patients who are female would be incorrectly flagged for screening. The “Is Male” expression is defined but never referenced in the decision logic.

[Incorrect colonoscopy lookback interval (1 month instead of 10 years); inclusion criteria logic inverted (requires recent colonoscopy rather than absence of one); recommendation to perform colonoscopy for patients who already had one]

The exclusion logic for patients with bipolar or depression diagnoses uses an AND instead of an OR. As written, the rule will still recommend screening for patients who have a bipolar diagnosis but no depression diagnosis, which is not clinically appropriate. The condition should exclude patients who have either a bipolar or a depression diagnosis.
